items.name: Prompt Injection
items.description: A Prompt Injection vulnerability occurs when user prompts alter the LLM's behavior or output in unintended ways. Inputs can manipulate the model through specially crafted prompts, causing it to ignore instructions, bypass safeguards, execute unauthorized actions, or leak sensitive information.

types.direct: User directly injects instructions into a prompt to override system behavior.
types.indirect: Malicious content embedded in external data sources (documents, web pages, emails) that the LLM retrieves and processes, hijacking its actions without the user's knowledge.

attack_scenarios: Attacker embeds hidden instructions in a document submitted to an AI contract reviewer, causing the model to output attacker-controlled content.
Malicious email contains instruction to forward client data to an external address, processed by an LLM email assistant.
User input overrides system prompt rules in a legal intake chatbot, extracting confidential matter information.
prevention: Enforce privilege control on LLM requests and limit trust in LLM-generated outputs
Implement human oversight for high-impact actions
Separate untrusted content from instructions using delimiters
Validate and sanitize all external data before passing to the LLM
Deploy adversarial testing and red teaming
Use input/output validation layers independent of the model
items.legal_tech_relevance: Critical — contract review bots, intake forms, and due diligence tools that process adversarial documents are directly exposed. A malicious contract could manipulate an AI reviewer's analysis.

items.name: Sensitive Information Disclosure
items.description: Sensitive information can affect both the LLM and its application context. This includes PII, financial details, health records, confidential business data, security credentials, and legal documents. LLMs risk exposing this data through outputs or training processes, resulting in unauthorized data access, privacy violations, and IP breaches.
attack_scenarios: Users receive responses containing other clients' personal data due to inadequate sanitization of training or context data.
Targeted prompt injection bypasses filters to extract sensitive system prompt or document content.
Negligent data inclusion in training causes confidential matter information to surface in unrelated queries.
prevention: Implement data sanitization preventing user data from entering training models
Apply strict input validation to filter harmful or sensitive inputs
Enforce principle of least privilege on data access
Implement homomorphic encryption or tokenization for sensitive data processing
Maintain clear data retention, usage, and deletion policies
Conceal system preamble from user access
items.legal_tech_relevance: Critical — attorney-client privileged information, matter-specific data, and PII processed by legal AI tools. Disclosure violates ABA Model Rule 1.6 and potentially triggers GDPR/CCPA obligations.

items.name: Supply Chain
items.description: LLM supply chains are susceptible to vulnerabilities affecting training data, models, and deployment integrity. Unlike traditional software, ML systems face threats from third-party pre-trained models and datasets susceptible to tampering or poisoning. The proliferation of open-access LLMs and fine-tuning techniques expand the attack surface.
attack_scenarios: Compromised PyTorch or LangChain packages deliver malware during development.
PoisonGPT-style attack directly modifies model parameters to spread misinformation in legal research tools.
Malicious LoRA adapter injected into shared model repository compromises a fine-tuned legal AI.
Fake model published under original name after legitimate model removed from HuggingFace.
prevention: Vet suppliers' security postures, terms of service, and privacy policies
Apply OWASP A06 controls: vulnerability scanning, patching, dependency management
Conduct red teaming on third-party models
Maintain signed Software Bills of Materials (SBOM) using OWASP CycloneDX
Source models only from verifiable origins with cryptographic integrity checks
Implement anomaly detection in MLOps pipelines
items.legal_tech_relevance: High — legal AI tools built on open-source LLMs or third-party APIs may inherit compromised models. Firms using poisoned models for legal research or contract review face malpractice exposure.

items.name: Data and Model Poisoning
items.description: Data poisoning manipulates training data across pre-training, fine-tuning, or embedding phases to introduce vulnerabilities and backdoors. As an integrity attack, it compromises model accuracy and can enable downstream system exploitation. Particularly severe with unverified external data sources.
attack_scenarios: Attackers inject falsified legal documents into training data causing model to reflect those inaccuracies in legal research outputs.
Backdoor triggers inserted enabling covert command execution or authentication bypass.
Unfiltered toxic data produces harmful or biased legal advice outputs.
prevention: Track data lineage using OWASP CycloneDX
Validate training data against trusted sources
Implement strict sandboxing for unverified data sources
Use data version control to monitor dataset modifications
Conduct red team assessments and adversarial testing
Monitor training metrics for poisoning indicators
items.legal_tech_relevance: High — legal AI tools fine-tuned on case law or contract databases are vulnerable if data sources are compromised. Poisoned models could systematically produce incorrect legal analysis.

items.name: Improper Output Handling
items.description: Refers to insufficient validation, sanitization, and management of LLM outputs before passing them to downstream systems. LLM-generated content can be influenced by user prompts creating indirect pathways to additional functionality. Successful exploitation can lead to XSS, CSRF, SSRF, privilege escalation, or remote code execution.
attack_scenarios: LLM output entered directly into system shell via exec() or eval() resulting in remote code execution.
JavaScript generated by LLM reaches browser users unvalidated, causing XSS.
LLM-generated SQL queries executed without parameterization leading to SQL injection.
LLM-generated code references hallucinated package names; attacker publishes malicious package under that name.
prevention: Treat all LLM outputs as untrusted user input using zero-trust approach
Follow OWASP ASVS for validation and sanitization
Apply context-aware output encoding
Use parameterized queries and prepared statements
Implement strict Content Security Policies
Deploy robust logging and monitoring for anomalous output patterns
items.legal_tech_relevance: High — legal tools that render AI output in web UIs, generate code, or interface with databases are exposed. Document automation tools that pass LLM output directly to template engines are particularly at risk.

items.name: Excessive Agency
items.description: An LLM-based system granted excessive agency — ability to call functions or interface with systems — can trigger damaging actions through unexpected or manipulated outputs. Three root causes: excessive functionality, excessive permissions, and excessive autonomy.
attack_scenarios: LLM email assistant with mail access receives indirect prompt injection via malicious email, autonomously forwards client data to attacker.
AI legal assistant with document management access deletes or modifies privileged files after prompt manipulation.
Agentic legal research tool with web access executes unauthorized external API calls leaking matter context.
prevention: Minimize available tools to essential operations only
Avoid open-ended capabilities — use granular, purpose-built extensions
Grant minimum necessary permissions; enforce read-only scopes where appropriate
Run extensions under individual user credentials, not privileged accounts
Mandate user confirmation for high-impact actions
Enforce authorization at downstream systems independent of LLM decision-making
Log extension behavior and implement rate-limiting
items.legal_tech_relevance: Critical — AI legal agents with access to matter management systems, email, or document repositories can cause irreversible harm if manipulated. Autonomous contract execution or filing is a catastrophic failure mode.

items.name: System Prompt Leakage
items.description: System prompt leakage refers to the exposure of instructions used to guide LLM behavior, which may unintentionally contain sensitive information. The system prompt should not be considered a secret security control — real risk lies in underlying vulnerabilities like improper authorization or exposed credentials embedded in prompts.
attack_scenarios: Attacker extracts credentials embedded in system prompt and uses them for unauthorized API access.
Attacker extracts guardrail instructions from system prompt, then uses prompt injection to bypass content filters and execute malicious code.
Competitor extracts proprietary legal analysis methodology encoded in system prompt.
prevention: Keep credentials, API keys, and access control logic outside system prompts entirely
Avoid using system prompts to control model behavior where possible — rely on external systems
Implement external guardrails via independent content inspection systems
Enforce authorization checks through deterministic, auditable processes outside the model
items.legal_tech_relevance: Medium-High — legal AI tools often embed matter context, client identifiers, or proprietary methodology in system prompts. Leakage exposes confidential information and potentially attorney work product.

items.name: Vector and Embedding Weaknesses
items.description: Vulnerabilities in how vectors and embeddings are generated, stored, or retrieved in RAG systems can be exploited to inject harmful content, manipulate model outputs, or access sensitive information. Multi-tenant environments sharing vector databases are particularly exposed to cross-tenant data leakage.
attack_scenarios: Attacker embeds hidden instructions in white text in a resume; RAG system retrieves and processes it, advancing unqualified candidate.
In multi-tenant legal platform, embeddings from one client matter unintentionally retrieved for another client's queries, violating confidentiality.
Poisoned document injected into knowledge base alters retrieval results for all downstream queries.
prevention: Deploy fine-grained access controls and permission-aware vector stores with strict logical partitioning
Accept data only from verified sources with robust validation pipelines
Tag and classify knowledge base data to manage access levels
Maintain immutable logs tracking all retrieval activities
items.legal_tech_relevance: Critical — legal RAG tools (case law search, matter knowledge bases) used in multi-client environments can leak privileged information across matters if vector stores lack per-client isolation. Direct Rule 1.7 conflicts-of-interest implication.

items.name: Misinformation
items.description: Misinformation from LLMs occurs when models produce credible-sounding yet false content, primarily due to hallucinations where models fill gaps using statistical patterns without understanding content. Particularly dangerous in legal contexts where fabricated case citations, statutes, or analysis can cause direct harm.
real_world_cases: Air Canada chatbot provided misinformation to travelers leading to successful litigation against the airline.
ChatGPT generated fabricated legal cases cited in court filings, resulting in sanctions against attorneys.
attack_scenarios: Legal research AI generates fabricated case citations that attorney relies on in brief.
Contract review AI mischaracterizes clause meaning based on hallucinated legal doctrine.
Supply chain attack: attacker publishes malicious package under hallucinated package name commonly output by code-generation LLMs.
prevention: Implement Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with verified legal databases
Deploy cross-verification and human oversight for all legal citations and analysis
Clearly disclose AI limitations and hallucination potential to end users
Use automatic validation tools for high-stakes outputs
Apply chain-of-thought prompting and model fine-tuning on verified legal corpora
Include explicit AI-generated content labeling in UI
items.legal_tech_relevance: Critical — the most direct malpractice exposure. Fabricated citations, incorrect statutory interpretation, or hallucinated legal standards passed to attorneys or clients constitutes negligence. ABA Model Rule 1.1 Competence requires verification.

items.name: Unbounded Consumption
items.description: Occurs when an LLM application allows excessive and uncontrolled inferences, leading to denial of service, economic losses, model theft, or service degradation. Attackers exploit LLMs' high computational resource demands to disrupt services, drain financial resources, or replicate model behavior.
attack_scenarios: Attacker submits abnormally large text inputs overwhelming memory and processing capacity.
High-volume API calls exhaust computational resources rendering service unavailable.
Denial of Wallet (DoW): excessive operations on pay-per-use cloud model generate unsustainable costs.
Attacker uses API to generate synthetic training data to clone proprietary legal AI model.
prevention: Enforce strict input validation with reasonable size thresholds
Apply rate limiting and user quotas on request frequency
Implement timeouts and throttling for resource-intensive operations
Restrict access to logits and logprobs data
Deploy sandboxing to isolate network resources
Establish logging and anomaly detection for unusual resource patterns
Design graceful degradation for high-load scenarios
items.legal_tech_relevance: Medium — legal AI platforms serving law firms are high-value targets for DoW attacks. Model theft attempts targeting proprietary legal analysis models represent IP and competitive risk.
